title: Agamemnon
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: A WATCHMAN. CLYTEMNESTRA, wife of Agamemnon. AGAMEMNON, King of Argos. CASSANDRA, a Trojan prophetess. AEGISTHUS, Clytemnestra's lover. CHORUS of Argive Elders.
PROLOGUE
(A WATCHMAN lies on the palace roof at Argos, watching for the beacon fire that will signal Troy's fall.)

WATCHMAN: I pray the gods to end this labor—this year-long watch I keep upon the palace roof. I know every star in the summer sky, and I wait for the signal: the fire that will cry across the dark the message: Troy has fallen.
(A beacon blazes.)

WATCHMAN: The light! The message is given! (He dances briefly, then stops.) But I rejoice—and yet I grieve. This house knows something I cannot speak openly. I have seen what I have seen. I will say nothing more.
(The CHORUS enters—old men of Argos.)

CHORUS: Ten years have passed since Agamemnon and Menelaus, twin lords of the sons of Atreus, sailed for Troy with a thousand ships, to win back Helen and take vengeance on Paris. We are too old to go to war; we stayed behind and wait. A great crime sets great punishment in motion—the law is ancient and inexorable.
EPISODE I
(CLYTEMNESTRA enters.)
CHORUS: What is the news? Why this sacrifice?
CLYTEMNESTRA: Joy! Troy is taken! Tonight, the city burns!
CHORUS: How can you know this? It is night. Troy is far away.
CLYTEMNESTRA: Fire carries the news—beacon to beacon, across the sea, from mountain to mountain, each fire kindled from the last. Troy to Argos in a single night. My husband is coming home.
CHORUS: A woman of wonderful resource.
CLYTEMNESTRA: (with a cold smile) Yes.
EPISODE II
(AGAMEMNON returns, victorious, with CASSANDRA as his prize.)
CHORUS: Welcome, welcome, son of Atreus!
AGAMEMNON: (to the city) I salute you, Argos, and you, my gods. I have come home. Troy is fallen. Justice has been done.
(CLYTEMNESTRA approaches with crimson tapestries.)

CLYTEMNESTRA: (to the servants) Spread the crimson in his path. Let justice guide his steps to a home he never hoped to see again. (To AGAMEMNON.) My lord—my husband—how I have waited. Come. Walk in over this.
AGAMEMNON: (hesitating at the crimson cloth) This is—this honor is for gods, not men. To walk on crimson, on fine-dyed cloth—this is hubris.
CLYTEMNESTRA: Achilles did not hesitate. Great Priam—

AGAMEMNON: (yielding) Very well. But let someone remove my sandals first. No man should walk in pride over God's creation. (He removes his sandals, walks in.) Cassandra—come inside. The gods have been good. Let us be kind to those we bring home.
(CLYTEMNESTRA leads him in. CASSANDRA remains outside.)

CASSANDRA: (in a prophetic frenzy) I see it! I see the blood! This house—this house is full of old blood—and new blood coming—I see the bath—I see the net—I see the ax—O Apollo! Why have you brought me here? I know this house. I know what has been done here and what will be done. (She calms herself.) I will go in. I will die. But he who kills me will die too. There is a son. Orestes. He will return. (She goes inside.)
(From inside, AGAMEMNON cries out.)
AGAMEMNON: (off) I am struck! Strike again!
(CLYTEMNESTRA appears, blood-stained, with an ax.)

CLYTEMNESTRA: I struck him twice, and with two cries he gave up his limbs. When he was down I gave a third blow—to Zeus of the Underworld, who keeps the dead. And here he lies. (A pause.) He poured out this black blood and sprinkled me with the dark drops of death's dew, and I rejoiced—as the sown corn rejoices in the blessing of rain at harvest. So things stand, you council of Argos—rejoice if you will, I glory in this deed.
CHORUS: What frenzy drove you to this? What will you say to the people?
CLYTEMNESTRA: Behold the man. He sacrificed his own child—my child, Iphigenia—to send the fleet to sea. He destroyed her. For ten years I have waited. Justice has been done. Now judge me as you will.

title: The Libation Bearers
sourceName: Aeschylus — The Oresteia: The Libation Bearers (458 BC)
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: ORESTES, son of Agamemnon. ELECTRA, his sister. CLYTEMNESTRA. AEGISTHUS. A NURSE. PYLADES, Orestes' companion. CHORUS of Captive Women.
(Years have passed. ORESTES returns to Argos in disguise, commanded by Apollo to avenge his father's death.)
ORESTES: (at Agamemnon's tomb) Father—hear me. I have come as Apollo commanded. I will punish those who murdered you. (He sees a procession approaching.) Hide—let me see who comes.
(ELECTRA leads women carrying libations to the tomb.)
ORESTES: (recognizing her) My sister! She is here—to mourn him.
(He steps forward.)
ELECTRA: Who are you? What do you want at my father's tomb?
ORESTES: Electra. Look at me. You do not know your own brother?
ELECTRA: (staring) You—Orestes? Can it truly be—
ORESTES: It is. I have come back. Apollo commanded it. Our father must be avenged.

ELECTRA: (with grief and fervor) I have prayed for this. I have prayed so long. Inside that house, with that woman—our mother—and her Aegisthus—I have had nothing. Nothing but my prayer that you would come.
ORESTES: Tell me: does she rule well?
ELECTRA: She rules in terror and blood. The household trembles. Everything that was our father's is hers and Aegisthus's now. Come—let us plan.
(ORESTES enters the palace in disguise and is received by CLYTEMNESTRA as a stranger bearing false news of Orestes' death.)
CLYTEMNESTRA: (hearing the news) Dead? My son is dead? (A pause—and the CHORUS watches her carefully.) News of this kind—it is a double wound: we mourn, and yet we may be safer.
(But ORESTES reveals himself. He drives AEGISTHUS ahead of him into the house.)
AEGISTHUS: (off) Mercy—
ORESTES: (off) There is no mercy.
(CLYTEMNESTRA confronts ORESTES.)
CLYTEMNESTRA: Stop. Look at this breast. Here is where you lay as a child. I nursed you. Will you kill the one who gave you life?
ORESTES: (wavering) Pylades—(to his companion)—should I spare her?
PYLADES: (the only words he speaks in the play) What of Apollo's oracle? Better the world's hatred than the gods' anger.
ORESTES: (to Clytemnestra) Come inside.
CLYTEMNESTRA: I think you mean to kill me.
ORESTES: You killed my father. What did you expect?
(They go inside. CLYTEMNESTRA cries out.)
(ORESTES appears with the bodies, holding the net that Clytemnestra used to trap Agamemnon.)
ORESTES: The murderers are dead. Justice is done. But—(He presses his hands to his head.) —something is coming—I see them—the Furies—the dog-faced ones—they come for me—
CHORUS: Be still—
ORESTES: They come for the matricide. They will not let me rest. Apollo—I will go to your shrine. I will beg purification. (He runs.)

title: The Eumenides
sourceName: Aeschylus — The Oresteia: The Eumenides (458 BC)
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: ORESTES. THE FURIES (Chorus). APOLLO. ATHENA. CITIZENS of Athens, serving as jury.
(ORESTES has fled to Apollo's shrine at Delphi, then to Athens. The FURIES pursue him.)
APOLLO: (to the FURIES) Leave this house. Go. This man is under my protection.
FURIES: Your protection? You helped him kill his mother. We do not answer to Olympian gods. We answer to the ancient law: blood for blood. Matricide cannot go unpunished.
APOLLO: The father is the true parent. The mother is merely the earth in which the seed is planted. Agamemnon—Orestes' father—was murdered. Orestes avenged him. Justice was done.
FURIES: We do not accept that argument.
(The trial takes place in Athens, before ATHENA and a jury of Athenian citizens.)

ATHENA: This is a case beyond my power to judge alone. It touches on things so deep—the claims of heaven and earth, old law and new—that I will summon a jury of citizens, and we will judge together. This is the first trial of its kind. Let it set the pattern for all time.
(The FURIES and APOLLO speak for either side. The jury votes and the votes are equal.)
ATHENA: When the votes are equal, I cast mine for acquittal. My reason: I side always with the father. This man avenged his father. Let him go free.
FURIES: (in fury) You have trampled our ancient rights—you Olympians—you younger gods—

ATHENA: No. I offer you something better than anger. Stay here in Athens. Take up residence beneath this sacred hill. You will be honored—great offerings, great reverence. You will have a share in everything. But you will be transformed: no longer the hound of vengeance, but the guardians of justice. The Eumenides—the Kindly Ones.
(A long silence among the FURIES.)
FURIES: (slowly) What you offer—(another silence)—we accept.

ATHENA: Then be gracious to this city. Bring fertility and peace to those who honor you. And let the law of blood that governed the ancient world give way to this new thing: the trial before a jury, the judgment of citizens, mercy balanced with justice.

CHORUS: (singing) Let kindness prevail. Let the dark powers be reconciled. Let there be neither strife nor civil war nor grief in this city. Let all joy be in common, and all love be shared, and let the law hold steady. These things we grant, who were the daughters of Night and who are now the daughters of the new order: the Eumenides, the Kindly Ones.
(All process in torchlight through the city.)
CURTAIN.

The Oresteia ends here—the first great trilogy of Western drama. The story that began with a fleet sailing for Troy and a woman waiting with an axe ends with the founding of justice: not vengeance endless and self-perpetuating, but judgment, argument, and the vote. The Furies become the Kindly Ones. The cycle of blood is broken. It is the beginning of something new.
